SUSE AI + ClearML
SUSE Technical Reference Documentation: Reference Configuration
The objective of this guide is to provide an opinionated technical architecture for a layered solution involving SUSE and partner components.  As with all SUSE documentation, this document should be well-structured, clear, and concise.  For a solution that involves only SUSE components, use the SUSE Technical Reference Documentation: Reference Configuration Template.
Consistent writing style and formatting contribute to the creation of high quality documentation.  Writing style goes beyond the essential elements of spelling, grammar, and punctuation to include tone of voice, choice of words, sentence and paragraph structure, and more.
Keep in mind the following general writing style guidance as you create your guide:
Make your content compelling, relevant, and valuable for your target audience.
Be clear, concise, and complete.
Employ a conversational yet professional tone.
Refer to the reader in the second person (you).
Write in the active voice and the present tense
Use inclusive language.
Use simple language and avoid jargon.
Avoid contractions and abbreviations.
Follow brand guidelines (see https://brand.suse.com).
Leverage Search Engine Optimization (SEO) and best practices to raise ranking by search engines and grow visibility.
Some additional considerations include:
Document procedures with clear steps using ordered lists, but do not exceed more than two levels.
Prefer commands over screenshots.
For graphical procedures, screenshots can be included, but also write out the procedure (e.g., “Select File > Save as in the menu”).
Use sections to organize the guide (some suggested sections are provided in this template), but use no more than two section levels.
Include admonitions (e.g., NOTE, IMPORTANT, WARNING) to provide additional context or highlight important information.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Introduction
This technical reference provides a validated design for building an enterprise-grade AI compute and machine learning operations (MLOps) platform using SUSE® AI integrated with ClearML. It describes an opinionated but flexible architecture, designed to help organizations deploy, operate, and scale machine learning workloads in a secure, consistent, and observable manner across on-premises, cloud, and hybrid environments.
As enterprises increasingly adopt artificial intelligence to drive business value, many face challenges in moving from isolated experimentation to reliable, repeatable production deployments. Common obstacles include fragmented tooling, inconsistent environments, limited lifecycle visibility, and difficulties in scaling GPU-enabled workloads while maintaining security and governance. SUSE AI and ClearML together address these challenges by providing a Kubernetes-native foundation for AI workloads combined with end-to-end MLOps capabilities.
This reference configuration focuses on platform-level concerns rather than data science methodologies. It outlines how platform teams can standardize machine learning workflows, manage shared AI infrastructure, and enable multiple data science teams to work efficiently within a governed and observable environment at enterprise scale. It includes discussion of business context and value as well as technical architecture and operational guidance.

sections.heading: Business aspect
sections.content:
Business aspect
Enterprises increasingly invest in AI infrastructure to support process automation, AI model development, and management. While many organizations succeed in acquiring powerful hardware, they often struggle to operate it efficiently, securely, and at scale.
Without a centralized infrastructure management layer, AI resources are frequently underutilized, fragmented across teams, and difficult to govern. Inconsistent access controls, limited visibility into usage, and ad hoc workload orchestration lead to poor utilization of expensive compute and increased operational overhead, resulting in lost ROI.
This reference configuration addresses these challenges by providing an enterprise-ready AI infrastructure management platform based on SUSE AI and ClearML. The solution enables organizations to operate shared compute as a controlled, multi-tenant platform while offering essential tools that support AI builders without requiring a separate MLOps stack.

sections.heading: Business problem
sections.content:
Business problem
Organizations attempting to scale machine learning commonly encounter the following challenges:
Limited visibility - Without centralized observability, teams struggle to understand resource allocation, workload placement, and actual compute consumption across the organization.
Infrastructure underutilization - Organizations invest heavily in AI compute but lack centralized control to ensure resources are efficiently shared, scheduled, and fully utilized across teams.
Operational complexity - Running AI workloads across multiple clusters and environments introduces manual processes, fragile configurations, and increased operational risk for platform teams.
Security and governance challenges - Shared AI infrastructure requires consistent enforcement of isolation, access control, and policies to protect sensitive data and intellectual property.
Scalability and resource flexibility - AI workloads demand elastic access to compute resources and require a uniform resource access interface across hybrid environments.
Multi-tenancy - Enterprise platforms must support multiple teams with shared infrastructure while maintaining isolation, resource governance, and access control.
Fragmented model development and deployment tooling - Teams need a consistent toolset to develop, test, and deploy models, but fragmented tools and workflows slow iteration and increase operational friction.
These challenges affect multiple personas, including data scientists, platform teams, IT operations, and business stakeholders responsible for risk and compliance.

sections.heading: Business value
sections.content:
Business value
This reference configuration addresses these challenges by providing a standardized, enterprise-ready MLOps platform that delivers the following benefits:
Accelerated time to value - Streamlined and repeatable machine learning workflows reduce friction from experimentation to production.
Improved operational consistency - A Kubernetes-native platform ensures consistent deployment and operation of ML workloads across environments, improving infrastructure ROI.
Enhanced security and governance - Integrated security and observability capabilities help protect models, data, and pipelines throughout the lifecycle, suitable for sovereign AI initiatives.
Scalable AI infrastructure - The solution supports growth by enabling elastic scaling of compute resources, including GPU-enabled workloads when required.
Future-ready architecture - A modular and cloud-agnostic design allows organizations to adapt to evolving AI use cases and infrastructure strategies.
This solution is designed as a strong foundation for operationalizing AI while maintaining control, visibility, and security at enterprise scale.

sections.heading: Architectural overview
sections.content:
Architectural overview
The solution, featuring the integration of SUSE AI and ClearML, provides a unified AI infrastructure platform that supports the full lifecycle of AI workloads, from experimentation and training through deployment and monitoring, while enforcing consistency, security, and operational visibility. Its architecture follows a layered, Kubernetes-native design that allows clear separation of responsibilities. This results in highly flexible, AI infrastructure that can scale across on-premises, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments.
At a high level, the architecture consists of the following layers:
Compute or hardware infrastructure
Container management
AI and MLOps platform
Security and observability
The following diagram illustrates the details of this architecture.
NOTE:
This reference configuration focuses on architectural patterns and integration points. For installation details and hardware-specific guidance, refer to the official SUSE and ClearML documentation.
The key components of the solution are:
Software
ClearML - Delivers AI infrastructure management capabilities that enable GPU optimization, AI workload orchestration and scheduling, policy-driven resource governance, and full lifecycle control of AI workloads and models.
SUSE® AI - Provides the enterprise-grade Kubernetes foundation and runtime environment for AI and machine learning workloads.
SUSE® Rancher Prime - Enables centralized Kubernetes cluster management, access control, and lifecycle operations.
SUSE® Security - Provides container image scanning, runtime security, and network visibility.
SUSE® Private Registry - Acts as the secure container image registry for training and inference workloads.
SUSE® Storage - Provides distributed, cloud-native persistent storage for platform services and AI workloads.
SUSE® Kubernetes Engine (RKE2) - Provide the secure Kubernetes orchestration layer to run platform services, training jobs, and inference workloads.
SUSE® Linux Enterprise Server - Provides the secure, stable, and enterprise-grade operating system foundation for Kubernetes nodes and supporting infrastructure. SUSE Linux Enterprise Server delivers a hardened Linux environment with long-term support, integrated security updates, and certified compatibility with SUSE AI, RKE2, and GPU drivers, ensuring consistent and reliable operation of AI platform services and workloads.
Compute platform - Supplies computational, networking, and storage hardware (whether on-premises, including air-gapped, or cloud) to support infrastructure and workload requirements.
Each component is further described in the next sections.

Software components

sections.heading: ClearML
sections.content:
ClearML
provides the AI infrastructure management and workload orchestration capabilities required to manage the AI training, building, and inferencing lifecycle. It integrates with SUSE AI to deliver infrastructure optimization, resource allocation, experiment tracking, pipeline orchestration, and model governance on top of the Kubernetes platform.
Within this reference design, ClearML is responsible for:
Tracking experiments, pipelines metrics, parameters, and models
Managing datasets and data lineage
Managing built-in multi-tenancy and billing
Optimizing infrastructure with dynamic, fractional GPUs and ClearML’s Unified Memory Technology
Defining resource quotas, over-quotas, priorities and spillovers
ClearML is comprised of three layers:
Infrastructure Control Plane - allowing you to manage and optimize AI infrastructure – whether on-premises, in the cloud, or both - ensuring high performance and cost optimization
AI Developer Center - providing a robust environment for developing, training, and testing AI models, accessible from anywhere
GenAI Application Engine - deploying and managing AI workloads and AI agents with pre-configured networking, authentication, and security
These layers are implemented by deploying three main components into the resource cluster:
ClearML Enterprise Server - control plane that provides back-end services and the UI
ClearML Agent - manages the orchestration of workloads on the Kubernetes cluster
ClearML Application Gateway - controls the networking, authentication and authorization into and out of ClearML-provisioned pods
ClearML operates as the primary interface for AI builders, while relying on SUSE AI for secure and scalable workload execution.

sections.content:
SUSE Kubernetes Engine (RKE2)
provides the secure, enterprise-ready Kubernetes distribution used to run all platform services and machine learning workloads in this architecture. It delivers a CNCF-compliant Kubernetes environment that is hardened by default and suitable for regulated and enterprise environments.
RKE2 is responsible for:
Providing the Kubernetes orchestration layer for SUSE AI and ClearML
Running platform services, training jobs, and inference workloads
Enforcing Kubernetes-native scheduling, networking, and resource isolation
Supporting GPU-enabled workloads where required
Integrating with SUSE Rancher for centralized lifecycle management
RKE2 forms the foundational orchestration layer for the platform and enables consistent workload execution across on-premises, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments.

sections.heading: SUSE Storage
sections.content:
SUSE Storage
, powered by Longhorn, provides distributed, cloud-native persistent storage for the platform. It delivers highly available and resilient storage that supports stateful platform services and machine learning workloads running on Kubernetes.
SUSE Storage is responsible for:
Storing ClearML metadata, artifacts, and logs
Providing persistent volumes for platform and workloads
Supporting data replication and high availability
Enabling snapshotting and backup of critical platform data
SUSE Storage is deployed on RKE2 clusters and backed by local or network-attached disks. It provides consistent, Kubernetes-native storage that supports performance, durability, and operational requirements across environments.

sections.heading: Compute platform details
sections.content:
Compute platform details
The solution is deployed on a production-grade, Kubernetes cluster. The underlying compute infrastructure may be based on virtual machines, bare-metal servers, or cloud-native infrastructure, provided it meets enterprise availability and performance requirements.
A baseline, production deployment should include the following considerations:
Kubernetes Control Plane
At least three control-plane nodes for high availability quorum
Application / Platform Nodes
At least three worker nodes on Kubernetes cluster to host platform services with high availability
Execution / Worker Nodes (ClearML Agents)
A minimum node count will be based on technical and business requirements, such as concurrency, SLAs, dedicated pools for CPU and GPU workloads, and so on.
Worker node sizing must account for:
CPU and memory requirements per job
GPU requirements (if applicable)
Dataset size and I/O throughput
Expected concurrent task execution
Additional considerations for enterprise environments with strict availability requirements include:
Multi-node control plane (minimum three nodes)
Replicated application services
Persistent storage with redundancy
Load-balanced ingress
Detailed resource requirements, node sizing, storage specifications, and scaling considerations for individual components and clusters are defined in their respective sections of this guide. However, final node counts and configurations must be derived from workload analysis and capacity planning.

A baseline, production deployment should include the following considerations:

Kubernetes Control Plane
At least three control-plane nodes for high availability quorum

Application / Platform Nodes
At least three worker nodes on Kubernetes cluster to host platform services with high availability

Execution / Worker Nodes (ClearML Agents)
A minimum node count will be based on technical and business requirements, such as concurrency, SLAs, dedicated pools for CPU and GPU workloads, and so on.
Worker node sizing must account for:
CPU and memory requirements per job
GPU requirements (if applicable)
Dataset size and I/O throughput
Expected concurrent task execution
Additional considerations for enterprise environments with strict availability requirements include:
Multi-node control plane (minimum three nodes)
Replicated application services
Persistent storage with redundancy
Load-balanced ingress

Detailed resource requirements, node sizing, storage specifications, and scaling considerations for individual components and clusters are defined in their respective sections of this guide. However, final node counts and configurations must be derived from workload analysis and capacity planning.

sections.heading: 5 Deployment
sections.content:
5 Deployment
This section provides a high-level overview for deploying ClearML into your SUSE AI environment. It describes deployment considerations for the major compute platform and software layers to form a functional platform for AI workloads. The solution is represented as a layered stack, where each layer depends on the services provided by the layer below it. Deployment is performed in a bottom-up sequence, ensuring that each foundational capability is in place before higher-level services are introduced.

sections.heading: 5.3 SUSE Kubernetes Engine (RKE2)
sections.content:
5.3 SUSE Kubernetes Engine (RKE2)
Preparation
Identify the appropriate, desired version of the Rancher Kubernetes Engine binary (for example vX.Y.Z) that includes the needed Kubernetes version by reviewing
the "Supported Rancher Kubernetes Engine Versions" associated with the respective  version from "Rancher Kubernetes Engine Downstream Clusters" section, or
the "Releases" on the  Web page.
On the target node with a default installation of SUSE Linux Enterprise Server operating system, log in to the node either as root or as a user with sudo privileges and enable the required container runtime engine
Then validate the container runtime engine is working
For the underlying operating system firewall service, either
enable and configure the necessary inbound  or
stop and completely disable the firewall service.
Deployment process
The primary steps for deploying this RKE2 are:
Note - Installing Rancher Kubernetes Engine requires a client system (i.e. admin workstation) that has been configured with kubectl.
Download the Rancher Kubernetes Engine binary according to the instructions on product  page, then follow the directions on that page, but with the following exceptions:
Create the cluster.yml file with the command rke config
Note - See product documentation for  and  for detailed examples and descriptions of the cluster.yml parameters.
It is recommended to create a unique SSH key for this Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster with the command ssh-keygen
Provide the path to that key for the option "Cluster Level SSH Private Key Path"
The option "Number of Hosts" refers to the number of hosts to configure at this time
Additional hosts can be added very easily after Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster creation
For this implementation it is recommended to configure one or three hosts
Give all hosts the roles of "Control Plane", "Worker", and "etcd"
Answer "n" for the option "Enable PodSecurityPolicy"
Update the cluster.yml file before continuing with the step "Deploying Kubernetes with RKE."
If a load balancer has been deployed for the Rancher Kubernetes Engine control-plane nodes, update the cluster.yml file before deploying Rancher Kubernetes Engine to include the IP address or FQDN of the load balancer. The appropriate location is under authentication.sans. For example:
Verify password-less SSH is available from the admin workstation to each of the cluster hosts as the user specified in the cluster.yml file
When ready to create the RKE cluster, execute:
rke up
After the rke up command completes, the RKE cluster will continue the Kubernetes installation process.
TIP
To monitor the progress of the RKE2 cluster installation:
Export the variable KUBECONFIG to the absolute path name of the kube_config_cluster.yml file.
export KUBECONFIG=~/rke-cluster/kube_config_cluster.yml
Run the command:
watch -c "kubectl get deployments -A"
The cluster deployment is complete when elements of all the deployments show at least ‘1’ as ‘AVAILABLE’.
Use CTRL+C to exit the watch loop after all deployment pods are running.
To address  and possible  to a multiple node cluster, etcd is enabled instead of using the default SQLite datastore.
Deployment Consideration
To ensure a stable, secure, and scalable Rancher Kubernetes Engine (RKE/RKE2) deployment, consider the following aspects:
Availability
For production deployments, use an odd number of control-plane/etcd nodes (minimum three) to maintain etcd quorum and prevent loss of cluster availability. When deploying multiple control-plane nodes, use an external load balancer to distribute traffic across the Kubernetes API servers.
Networking
Ensure that all required Kubernetes, etcd, and container runtime ports are open between cluster nodes. Latency and packet loss between nodes—especially etcd members—can negatively impact cluster stability and performance.
etcd Data Protection
etcd stores all Kubernetes cluster state and configuration. Enable regular etcd snapshots and store backups outside the cluster to support disaster recovery and cluster restoration.
Security
Restrict SSH access to cluster nodes using key-based authentication only. Use least-privilege access for the SSH user specified in the cluster.yml file. Protect the generated kube_config_cluster.yml file, as it provides administrative access to the cluster.
Scalability
Additional worker or control-plane nodes can be added to the cluster after initial deployment by updating the cluster.yml file and re-running the rke up command. Plan IP addressing, DNS, and load balancer capacity accordingly.
Certificate Management
RKE automatically generates and manages Kubernetes certificates. If a load balancer or external endpoint is used, ensure all required IP addresses and FQDNs are included under authentication.sans in the cluster.yml file to avoid certificate validation issues.
Operating System Configuration
Disable swap and ensure required kernel modules are loaded on all nodes. Apply consistent OS configuration across nodes to avoid unpredictable behavior.
Upgrade Strategy
Before upgrading Kubernetes or RKE versions, review the supported version matrix and perform upgrades in a test environment. Back up etcd data prior to any upgrade operation.
Logging and Monitoring
Plan for cluster-level monitoring and logging early in the deployment lifecycle to simplify troubleshooting and operational visibility.

sections.heading: 5.4 SUSE Rancher Prime
sections.content:
5.4 SUSE Rancher Prime
Preparation
For the respective node’s firewall service, either
enable and configure the necessary inbound  or
stop and completely disable the firewall service.
Determine the desired  for TLS termination
Rancher-generated TLS certificate NOTE: This is the easiest way of installing SUSE Rancher with self-signed certificates.
Let’s Encrypt
Bring your own certificate
Obtain a  binary matching the respective Kubernetes version for this SUSE Rancher implementation.
Note - Enable the respective kubeconfig setting for kubectl , K3s - /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yml, to be leveraged by helm command.
Deployment Process
While logged in to the node, as root or with sudo privileges, install SUSE Rancher:
Install cert-manager
Set the following variable with the desired version of cert-manager
Note - At this time, the most current, supported version of cert-manager is v1.5.1
Create the cert-manager CRDs and apply the Helm Chart resource manifest
Check the progress of the installation, looking for all pods to be in running status:
Add the SUSE Rancher helm chart repository:
Create a namespace for SUSE Rancher
Prepare to use the Helm Chart for SUSE Rancher:
Set the following variable to the host name of the SUSE Rancher server instance
Note - This host name should be resolvable to an IP address of the K3s host, or a load balancer/proxy server that supports this installation of SUSE Rancher.
Set the following variable to the number of deployed K3s nodes planned to host the SUSE Rancher service
Set the following variable to the desired version of SUSE Rancher server instance
Install the SUSE Rancher Helm Chart
Monitor the progress of the installation:
(Optional) Create an SSH tunnel to access SUSE Rancher:
Note - This optional step is useful in cases where NAT routers and/or firewalls prevent the client Web browser from reaching the exposed SUSE Rancher server IP address and/or port. This step requires that a Linux host is accessible through SSH from the client system and that the Linux host can reach the exposed SUSE Rancher service. The SUSE Rancher host name should be resolvable to the appropriate IP address by the local workstation.
Create an SSH tunnel through the Linux host to the IP address of the SUSE Rancher server on the NodePort, as noted in Step 3:
On the local workstation Web browser, change the SOCKS Host settings to "127.0.0.1" and port "8080".
Note - This will route all traffic from this Web browser through the remote Linux host. Be sure to close the tunnel and revert the SOCKS Host settings when you are done.
Connect to the SUSE Rancher Web UI:
On a client system, use a Web browser to connect to the SUSE Rancher service, from HTTPs.
Provide a new Admin password.
Important - On the second configuration page, ensure the "Rancher Server URL" is set to the host name specified when installing the SUSE Rancher Helm Chart and the port is 443.
Deployment Consideration
To further optimize deployment factors, leverage the following practices
Availability - In instances where a load balancer is used to access a RKE2 cluster, deploying two additional RKE2 cluster nodes, for a total of three, will automatically make SUSE Rancher highly available.
Security - The basic deployment steps described above are for deploying SUSE Rancher with automatically generated, self-signed security certificates. Other options are to have SUSE Rancher create public certificates from Let’s Encrypt associated with a publicly resolvable host name for the SUSE Rancher server, or to provide preconfigured, private certificates.
Integrity - This deployment of SUSE Rancher uses the RKE2 etcd key/value store to persist its data and configuration, which offers several advantages. With a multi-node cluster and this resiliency through replication, having to provide highly-available storage is not needed. In addition, backing up the RKE2 etcd store protects the cluster and the installation of SUSE Rancher and permits restoration of a given state.
After this successful deployment of the SUSE Rancher solution, review the  for details on how downstream Kubernetes clusters can be deployed

sections.heading: 5.6 SUSE Storage
sections.content:
5.6 SUSE Storage
SUSE Storage provides persistent, software-defined storage services for containerized and cloud-native workloads. In this solution stack, SUSE Storage is used to supply highly available, scalable persistent volumes for Kubernetes workloads managed by SUSE Rancher.
Preparation
Before deploying SUSE Storage, ensure the following  are met:
A running Kubernetes cluster deployed using SUSE Kubernetes Engine (RKE2) and managed by SUSE Rancher
Nodes designated for storage meet the minimum hardware requirements:
Sufficient local disks (HDD or SSD) dedicated exclusively to storage use
Network connectivity with low latency between storage nodes
Time synchronization (NTP) is enabled and consistent across all nodes
Firewall rules allow required inbound and outbound traffic for SUSE Storage components
Note - Storage devices intended for SUSE Storage must not contain existing partitions or file systems.
Tip -  For production deployments, it is recommended to use three or more storage nodes to achieve quorum and high availability.
Deployment Process
The high-level steps for deploying SUSE Storage are as follows:
Deploy SUSE Storage through the SUSE Rancher UI:
Log in to the SUSE Rancher Web UI
Navigate to Apps
Select the supported SUSE Storage application (for example, SUSE Storage powered by Longhorn or Ceph, depending on the solution design)
Configure the storage cluster:
Select the target Kubernetes cluster
Assign storage roles to appropriate worker nodes
Specify disk devices to be used for storage
Configure replication and fault-tolerance settings
Install the SUSE Storage application:
Review and confirm the  configuration values
Deploy the application and monitor the installation progress
Verify the deployment:
Ensure all storage-related pods are running
Confirm that storage pools and volumes are successfully created
Validate persistent volume provisioning through a test workload
Deployment Consideration
To optimize the storage layer, consider the following:
High Availability - Deploy storage services across multiple nodes and failure domains to ensure resilience against node or disk failures.
Performance - Select appropriate disk types (SSD vs HDD) and configure replication factors based on workload performance requirements.
Capacity Planning - Monitor storage utilization and plan for future expansion by adding disks or nodes as needed.
Backup and Recovery - Enable snapshotting and backup mechanisms to protect persistent data and allow recovery from failures.

sections.heading: 5.7 SUSE Security
sections.content:
5.7 SUSE Security
SUSE Security provides runtime container and Kubernetes security, vulnerability scanning, continuous compliance checks, and policy enforcement for applications running on Kubernetes. This section describes how to deploy SUSE Security using the Rancher UI in a reproducible, step-by-step way.
Preparation
Before deploying, confirm the following:
Managed Kubernetes cluster (e.g., RKE2) is registered in SUSE Rancher
The Rancher server and cluster nodes have network access to pull images (internet or private registry)
Cluster has sufficient CPU, memory, and network resources for security components
Kubernetes namespace cattle-neuvector-system will be used for SUSE Security
Note: If deploying SUSE Security with Rancher Extensions (Rancher Prime), the integration supports single sign-on and deeper UI features; for community editions, Apps & Marketplace or Helm install is typical.
Deployment Process

sections.heading: 5.8 SUSE AI
sections.content:
5.8 SUSE AI
SUSE AI provides a cloud-native, scalable AI platform for deploying GenAI workloads on Kubernetes. It consists of a collection of modular components (AI middleware, vector databases, inference engines, etc.) that can be installed individually or as a full stack using the SUSE Deployer meta-chart. SUSE AI integrates with SUSE Rancher and conforms to SUSE software entitlement and cluster requirements.
Preparation
Ensure the following prerequisites are completed before deploying SUSE AI:
Cluster readiness
A running Kubernetes cluster (such as SUSE Rancher Prime: RKE2) with all nodes registered in Rancher.
GPU-enabled Nodes with NVIDIA drivers and CUDA Installed along with Node Labels for AI workloads. (reference - )
Entitlement and access
A valid SUSE AI subscription/entitlement is in place.
Access to the SUSE Application Collection repository to fetch Helm charts and container images.
Namespace
Create a dedicated Kubernetes namespace for SUSE AI workloads (e.g., suse-ai).
Registry credentials
Create a Kubernetes secret for the SUSE Application Collection registry, using your entitlement credentials.
Helm registry access
Log in to the Helm OCI registry holding SUSE AI charts.
cert-manager
Ensure cert-manager is installed on the cluster (required by certain AI components).
Deployment Process
Deploying SUSE AI involves installing each AI component (e.g., Ollama, Milvus, vLLM, Open WebUI, etc.) using the unified SUSE Deployer chart which orchestrates all components and dependencies in one step.
Prepare override values
Create a custom-overrides.yaml file based on one of the  (e.g., suse-gen-ai.yaml for production or suse-gen-ai-minimal.yaml for test).
Install with Helm
Use Helm to install the SUSE Deployer chart with your override file:
Monitor progress
Watch the status of AI workloads and pods via kubectl get pods -n suse-ai until all are in a running state.
Deployment Consideration
Entitlement and Registry Access
Access to the SUSE Application Collection registry is essential for pulling Helm charts and container images. Ensure credentials are current.
Order of Installation
Install components in dependency order: cert-manager first, then core AI services, then optional components such as PyTorch or GPU-based services.
Resource Planning
AI components (especially vector databases and inference workloads) can be resource intensive. Plan CPU, memory, storage, and GPU capacity accordingly.
Namespace Isolation
Use a dedicated Kubernetes namespace (e.g., suse-ai) to isolate SUSE AI workloads.
Monitoring & Logging
Integrate with SUSE Observability for metrics and logs to monitor performance and troubleshoot issues.

sections.heading: 3. Networking
sections.content:
3. Networking
The following network components need to be provisioned to make the ClearML control plane accessible:
Ingress Controller
An Ingress controller (e.g., nginx-ingress) is required. If exposing services externally, configure a LoadBalancer-capable solution (e.g. MetalLB).
Port specification
The designated nodes for the ClearML control plane servers and workers should be able to communicate through HTTP/S: TCP ports 80 and 443.
Any additional ports to be used in the deployment (i.e.  TCP sessions) should also be pre-configured (see ).
DNS Configuration
A domain with subdomain support is required (referred here-on as <BASE_DOMAIN>), ideally with trusted TLS certificates. All entries must be resolvable by the Ingress controller. Example subdomains:
Control plane servers:
api.<BASE_DOMAIN>
app.<BASE_DOMAIN>
files.<BASE_DOMAIN>
Application Gateway:
router.<BASE_DOMAIN>
tcp-router.<BASE_DOMAIN> (optional, for TCP sessions)
[For Advanced usage] CNI preconfigured
[For Multi-tenant deployment]  set up per tenant.

sections.heading: Multi-tenant configuration
sections.content:
Multi-tenant configuration
Establish an initial supervisor tenant with a supervisor user. This user will have the authority to create and remove other tenants, and access information across all tenants.
Update the clearml-values.override.yaml with the following values:
Replace the below <SUPERVISOR_COMPANY_ID>, <SUPERVISOR_USER_KEY>, <SUPERVISOR_USER_SECRET> and <SUPERVISOR_USER_EMAIL> with desired values. The <SUPERVISOR_USER_KEY> and <SUPERVISOR_USER_SECRET> can be used to log in as the supervisor user from the ClearML Web UI via app.<BASE_DOMAIN>.
Note that the <SUPERVISOR_USER_EMAIL> value must be explicitly quoted (e.g. "\"\"")
apiserver:
extraEnvs:
- name: CLEARML__services__organization__features__user_management_advanced
value: "true"
- name: CLEARML__services__workers__resource_usages__supervisor_company
value: "<SUPERVISOR_COMPANY_ID>"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__role
value: "system"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__allow_login
value: "true"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__user_key
value: "<SUPERVISOR_USER_KEY>"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__user_secret
value: "<SUPERVISOR_USER_SECRET>"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__sec_groups
value: "[\"users\", \"admins\", \"queue_admins\"]"
- name: CLEARML__secure__credentials__supervisor__email
value: "\"<SUPERVISOR_USER_EMAIL>\""
- name: CLEARML__apiserver__company__unique_names
value: "true"

sections.heading: Creating a Tenant
sections.content:
Creating a Tenant
Set up the environment for configuring the server via API:
The environment variables should match the values defined in the previous installation steps.
APISERVER_URL="https://api.<BASE_DOMAIN>"
APISERVER_KEY="<SUPERVISOR_USER_KEY>"
APISERVER_SECRET="<SUPERVISOR_USER_SECRET>"

Create a tenant:
Replace the below <TENANT_NAME> with a name for the new tenant
curl $APISERVER_URL/system.create_company \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-u $APISERVER_KEY:$APISERVER_SECRET \
-d '{"name":"<TENANT_NAME>"}'

The API returns the new tenant ID (<COMPANY_ID>)
Validate tenant creation by listing existing tenants:
curl -u $APISERVER_KEY:$APISERVER_SECRET $APISERVER_URL/system.get_companies

Create a utility administrator user for to facilitate tenant configuration:
Replace the below <COMPANY_ID> with the value returned when creating the tenant.
Replace the below <ADMIN_USER_NAME> and <ADMIN_USER_EMAIL> with suitable values.
curl $APISERVER_URL/auth.create_user \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-u $APISERVER_KEY:$APISERVER_SECRET \
-d '{"name":"<ADMIN_USER_NAME>","company":"<COMPANY_ID>","email":"<ADMIN_USER_EMAIL>","role":"admin","internal":"true"}'

Create a utility API credentials for this administrator user:
curl $APISERVER_URL/auth.create_credentials \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-H "X-Clearml-Impersonate-As: <USER_ID>" \
-u $APISERVER_KEY:$APISERVER_SECRET
The returned set of key and secret can be used for additional API configuration of the tenant.

sections.heading: 9. Setting up Worker Nodes
sections.content:
9. Setting up Worker Nodes
The ClearML Agent is installed on Kubernetes using a Helm chart. This sets up a controller pod that listens to ClearML queues and launches jobs as needed. The Agent can be deployed on the same Kubernetes cluster as the ClearML Control Plane or on a separate one.
The Controller pod:
Pulls jobs from ClearML execution queues
Prepares a Kubernetes job based on the provided YAML template.
Inside each job pod, the ClearML Agent:
Installs the required environment for the task.
Executes and monitors the task process.
Logs task data to the ClearML Server

sections.heading: [Optional]NVIDIA GPU Operator
sections.content:
[Optional]NVIDIA GPU Operator
When using Nvidia GPUs, users should configure the Nvidia GPU Operator according to recommended security settings, with ClearML’s overrides file.
Add the NVIDIA GPU Operator Helm repository:
helm repo add nvidia https://nvidia.github.io/gpu-operator

Update the local repository:
helm repo update

Create a gpu-operator.override.yaml file with Nvidia GPU configuration settings:
(These values were chosen to prevent unprivileged containers from bypassing the Kubernetes Device Plugin API):
toolkit:
env:
- name: ACCEPT_NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES_ENVVAR_WHEN_UNPRIVILEGED
value: "false"
- name: ACCEPT_NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES_AS_VOLUME_MOUNTS
value: "true"
devicePlugin:
env:
- name: PASS_DEVICE_SPECS
value: "true"
- name: FAIL_ON_INIT_ERROR
value: "true"
- name: DEVICE_LIST_STRATEGY # Use volume-mounts
value: volume-mounts
- name: DEVICE_ID_STRATEGY
value: uuid
- name: NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
value: all
- name: NVIDIA_DRIVER_CAPABILITIES
value: all

Install the Nvidia GPU Operator:
helm install -n gpu-operator gpu-operator nvidia/gpu-operator --create-namespace -f gpu-operator.override.yaml

ClearML supports various silicon vendors such as Nvidia, AMD and Intel. For detailed configuration instructions refer to ClearML’s .

- name: DEVICE_ID_STRATEGY
value: uuid

value: all

sections.content:
10. ClearML Agent Deployment
Add the ClearML Helm repository to your cluster:
helm repo add clearml-enterprise https://raw.githubusercontent.com/clearml/clearml-enterprise-helm-charts/gh-pages --username <HELM_REPO_TOKEN> --password <HELM_REPO_TOKEN>

Update the local repository:
helm repo update

Create a clearml-agent-values.override.yaml file with the agent settings:
Replace the below <ACCESS_KEY> and <SECRET_KEY> with the tenant admin API credentials created in the .
Set the <api|file|web>ServerUrlReference fields to match your ClearML Server URLs i.e. https://api.<BASE_DOMAIN> etc.
Replace <MAX_AVAILABLE_GPUS> with the total number of GPUs made available to ClearML agents on this cluster
imageCredentials:
password: "<CLEARML_DOCKERHUB_TOKEN>"
clearml:
  agentk8sglueKey: "<ACCESS_KEY>"
  agentk8sglueSecret: "<SECRET_KEY>"
agentk8sglue:
  apiServerUrlReference: "<CLEARML_API_SERVER_REFERENCE>"
  fileServerUrlReference: "<CLEARML_FILE_SERVER_REFERENCE>"
  webServerUrlReference: "<CLEARML_WEB_SERVER_REFERENCE>"
  createQueues: true
  queues:
    exampleQueue:
      templateOverrides: {}
      queueSettings: {}
  dashboardReportMaxGpu: <MAX_AVAILABLE_GPUS>
For more advanced configurations, refer to ClearML’s
Install the ClearML Enterprise Agent:
helm upgrade -i -n clearml-worker clearml-agent clearml-enterprise/clearml-enterprise-agent --create-namespace -f clearml-agent-values.override.yaml

sections.heading: 11. Setting up an Application Gateway
sections.content:
11. Setting up an Application Gateway
The Application Gateway enables access to AI workload applications (e.g. remote IDEs like VSCode and Jupyter, model API interface, etc.). It acts as a proxy, identifying ClearML Tasks running within its Kubernetes namespace and making them available for network access
In a multi-tenant deployment, tenant isolation is achieved by creating a dedicated Kubernetes namespace  for each tenant, and deploying a dedicated ClearML Application Gateway  for each tenant (using a tenant admin’s API credentials)

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.content:
Procedure
Add the ClearML Helm repository to your cluster:
helm repo add clearml-enterprise https://raw.githubusercontent.com/clearml/clearml-enterprise-helm-charts/gh-pages --username <HELM_REPO_TOKEN> --password <HELM_REPO_TOKEN>

Update the local repository:
helm repo update

Create a clearml-app-gateway-values.override.yaml file with the agent settings in the following form (See configuration description below):
imageCredentials:
password: "<CLEARML_DOCKERHUB_TOKEN>"
clearml:
apiKey: "<ACCESS_KEY>"
apiSecret: "<SECRET_KEY>"
apiServerUrlReference: "<CLEARML_API_SERVER_REFERENCE>"
authCookieName: ""
ingress:
enabled: true
hostName: ""
tcpSession:
routerAddress: ""
service:
type: LoadBalancer
portRange:
start:
end:

Set the <apiServerUrlReference> field to match your
Configuration options:
imageCredentials.password: ClearML DockerHub Access Token.
clearml.apiKey and clearml.apiSecret: Tenant admin API credentials created in the .
clearml.apiServerUrlReference: ClearML Server URL i.e. https://api.<BASE_DOMAIN>
clearml.authCookieName: Cookie used by the ClearML server to store the ClearML authentication cookie.
ingress.hostName: Hostname of App Gateway used by the ingress controller to access it.
tcpSession.routerAddress: The external App Gateway address (can be an IP, hostname, or load balancer address) depending on your network setup. Ensure this address is accessible for TCP connections.
tcpSession.service.type: Service type used to expose TCP functionality, default is NodePort.
tcpSession.portRange.start: Start port for the TCP Session feature.
tcpSession.portRange.end: End port for the TCP Session feature.
Install the ClearML Application Gateway:
Replace <RELEASE_NAME> with a Unique name for the App Gateway within the Kubernetes namespace. This is a required parameter in Helm, which identifies a specific installation of the chart. The release name also defines the App Gateway's name and appears in the UI within AI workload application URLs (e.g. Remote IDE URLs). This can be customized to support multiple installations within the same namespace by assigning different release names.
Replace <CHART_VERSION> - Version recommended by the ClearML Support Team.
helm upgrade --install <RELEASE_NAME> -n clearml-worker clearml-enterprise/clearml-enterprise-app-gateway --version <CHART_VERSION> -f clearml-app-gateway-values.override.yaml
		Verifying the installation
To verify the ClearML Agent has been deployed to your cluster:
Use a web browser to access https://app.<BASE_DOMAIN>
Log in as the initial administrator configured in the
Go to the Setting > Application Gateway > Routers page
The deployed <RELEASE_NAME> router will appear in the list.
Deployment considerations

sections.heading: SUSE Private Registry
sections.content:
SUSE Private Registry
SUSE Registry provides an enterprise-grade container image registry for storing, scanning, and managing container images used by Kubernetes workloads.
 In this reference configuration, SUSE Registry is deployed on the SUSE Kubernetes Engine and backed by SUSE Storage for persistent data.
SUSE Registry enables:
Secure image storage
Role-based access control
Image signing and vulnerability scanning
Integration with Rancher and CI/CD pipelines

sections.heading: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
sections.content:
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
Does ClearML support AMD accelerators?
Yes! ClearML is a silicon agnostic platform and supports accelerators from Nvidia, AMD, Intel and others. ClearML also supports on Intel and arm CPUs.
Any Examples for Training Models on top of ClearML?
Absolutely! You can find simple examples such as training an MNIST classifier with Pytorch  or fine tune an LLM using managed Q&A dataset . 
For all examples check out ClearML  in ClearML’s
Does ClearML support serving Nvidia NIMs containers
Yes, ClearML offers built-in deployment and management capabilities for Nvidia NIMs containers. It requires the NIMs application to be installed on the server.
Is ClearML installed on-prem?
ClearML supports installing both on-prem as well as on Cloud instances
Does ClearML support air-gapped deployments?
Yes, ClearML fully supports air-gapped deployments with no connection to the internet.
